coredata.dc:description: Owing to their small size, and unique physical and chemical properties, zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) have been increasingly critically analyzed for their potential applications in the fields of biology, chemistry, food science, and energy. This study systematically explores the multifunctional capabilities of CDs, emphasizing recent advancements in their synthesis techniques, precise characterization methods, and their tailored applications across interdisciplinary domains. The applications of CDs, which are generally smaller than 10 nm, in myriad fields are ascribed to their intrinsic merits of good biocompatibility, little cytotoxicity, good stability, and large specific surface area. The development of CDs along with their accurate characterization, and their successful implementation into faster, less expensive, and more reliable systems in a variety of scientific fields are testimony to how science has advanced and merged to create interdisciplinary fields. Despite extensive research, there is still the need to overcome the problem of large-scale production and improve quantum yield as well as functionality for specific applications, which this study seeks to analyze. Thus, a new era in nanotechnology has begun with the advancement in the exploration of CDs for applications in new and emerging scientific fields. CDs exhibit a huge number of distinctive physiochemical, photophysical, and photochemical properties which makes them a suitable platform for applications related to imaging, environmental remediation, catalysis, biology, and energy. The present research, therefore, focuses on the function and importance of CDs, as well as their synthesis, properties, and new developments in several fields of application.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Owing to their small size, and unique physical and chemical properties, zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) have been increasingly critically analyzed for their potential applications in the fields of biology, chemistry, food science, and energy. This study systematically explores the multifunctional capabilities of CDs, emphasizing recent advancements in their synthesis techniques, precise characterization methods, and their tailored applications across interdisciplinary domains. The applications of CDs, which are generally smaller than 10 nm, in myriad fields are ascribed to their intrinsic merits of good biocompatibility, little cytotoxicity, good stability, and large specific surface area. The development of CDs along with their accurate characterization, and their successful implementation into faster, less expensive, and more reliable systems in a variety of scientific fields are testimony to how science has advanced and merged to create interdisciplinary fields. Despite extensive research, there is still the need to overcome the problem of large-scale production and improve quantum yield as well as functionality for specific applications, which this study seeks to analyze. Thus, a new era in nanotechnology has begun with the advancement in the exploration of CDs for applications in new and emerging scientific fields. CDs exhibit a huge number of distinctive physiochemical, photophysical, and photochemical properties which makes them a suitable platform for applications related to imaging, environmental remediation, catalysis, biology, and energy. The present research, therefore, focuses on the function and importance of CDs, as well as their synthesis, properties, and new developments in several fields of application.

ce:section.ce:para: In response to global warming, energy crisis, and environmental pollution, the development of clean and renewable energy systems is more urgent and important than ever. In this regard, carbon‐based materials with different structures and morphologies have been explored and used in energy technologies due to their high abundance in nature, low cost, high conductivity, high chemical/thermal stabilities, high specific surface area, and so forth [1].

Owing to their microscopic size, nanomaterials exhibit unique chemical, thermal, and optical characteristics which makes them more attractive for a large number of applications. Many industries across the globe use toxic, non-biodegradable metal and metal oxide NPs. Many fields use surface-functionalized NPs. Biocompatible, highly dispersible nanomedicines made from modified biophysical NPs provide superior protection, lesser immunity, and more solubility. Ancient farmers have used metal and non-metal particles to prevent microbial growth and protect crops from fungal and bacterial diseases. Although pesticides treat plant diseases, their widespread use affects the environment by making antibiotics and antibacterials. Besides antibiotics, nanoparticles (NPs) permeabilize bacterial membranes. Semiconductors, cationic conjugated polymers, dehydrogel peptides, noble metals, graphene oxide, carbon tube-filled graphene layers, element-doped carbon NPs breakdown, and germ resistance in antibacterial NPs may impair health [2–4].

Carbon Dots (CDs) are distinguished among other carbon-based nanomaterials by their exceptional physicochemical properties, which enable them as the most promising candidates in the challenge of energy storage, biomedicine, environmental remediation, and optical sensing [5]. CDs are discrete quasi-spherical fluorescent nanomaterials with dimensions less than 10 nm. A novel framework for nanocarbon emitters, in contrast to fullerenes or carbon nanotubes, is established by their isotropic structure, ultrafine dimensions, and facile synthesis. At present, CDs are the subject of considerable interest on account of their distinctive attributes, which include photoluminescence that can be adjusted, minimal toxicity, and favorable biocompatibility. These photoactive nanomaterials do not flicker and are physically and photochemically stable. These characteristics render them exceedingly well-liked for a wide range of bioimaging applications, including in vitro and in vivo multicolor and one- or two-photon imaging. In addition, the hydrophilic nature of CDs and the abundance of oxygen-containing functional groups on their surfaces make them a promising material for the development of optical sensors that can detect environmental pollutants and biomolecules quantitatively. CDs encompass a diverse range of fluorescent carbon materials at the nanoscale. These materials are formally categorized as carbonized polymer dots, graphene quantum dots, carbon quantum dots, and carbon nanodots, based on the properties and specific carbon core structure.

Thus, due to their easy surface functionalization, biodegradability, and lack of toxicity, carbon particles, or more precisely carbon nanomaterials, have garnered a lot of attention. Because of their unique physicochemical properties and diverse applications, carbon-based nanostructures such as graphene, fullerenes, and carbon nanotubes have attracted a lot of attention. Due to their unique set of advantageous characteristics, CDs, a developing member of the "carbon family" and small carbon NPs smaller than 10 nm in size, are quickly gaining attention in the field of materials chemistry. In contrast to the carbon nanostructures mentioned above, CDs have a wealth of photoluminescence (PL) and photoelectrochemical characteristics. As carbon-based fluorescent NPs, the CDs can be broadly classified into several subgroups, including carbonized polymer dots, amorphous CDs, graphene dots, and graphite dots. CDs have been a helpful, affordable, and widely available material in the nanocarbon dot community lately. Their increasing popularity has led to much research because of their great potential for a wide range of technological applications. CDs are a new type of luminous nanostructure consisting of a carbon network created by an unclear carbon framework. These particles are inexpensive and possess good aqueous stability, photostability, tunable color emission, strong fluorescence, and facile surface functionalization. The present review emphasizes the different characteristics and the latest developments in CD applications [6–8].

Fluorescent carbon nanoparticles were first identified in 2004 by Xu et al. during the electrophoretic purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes [9]. Sun et al. introduced the name "carbon dots" in 2006 when they described creating a fluorescent carbon nanoparticle measuring less than 10 nm in size [10]. Subsequently, extensive study has been conducted to investigate the optical characteristics of CDs for several applications. CDs are nanoparticles that are nearly spherical, ranging in diameter from 2 to 10 nm, and exhibit fluorescence as their unique characteristics [11,12]. The structure consists of an amorphous to crystalline core primarily composed of sp2-carbon, with lattice spacings resembling graphitic or turbostratic carbon [13]. CDs that comprise a crystal lattice are frequently denoted as carbon quantum dots. Significant quantities of oxygen, nitrogen, and hydrogen are present in CDs as functional groups, which contribute to their exceptional solubility in water and facile functionalization [14]. The existing body of research has predominantly demonstrated that the excitation of CDs affects their photoluminescence, which is attributed to functional groups that generate energy traps at different energy levels. Furthermore, via excitation-independent CDs, surface-passivation of the surface energy state will result in the radiative transition of sp2 carbon as a single transition mode. By enhancing the radiative recombination of surface-trapped excitons, surface passivation stabilizes energy traps, leading to increased PL emissions and safeguarding CDs against quench reagents and environmental influences [15].

Despite the significant research conducted on Carbon Dots (CDs), no previous review has explored their multifunctional potential by integrating advances in synthesis techniques, photophysical properties, and their diverse applications across emerging fields such as sustainable energy, environmental remediation, and biomedicine. Unlike previous studies that have mainly focused on isolated aspects of CDs [16], this review offers a holistic view, critically analyzing recent developments and identifying underexplored opportunities for future research. This novel approach focuses on filling gaps in this literature and provides a unified approach to understanding the transformative role played by CDs in interdisciplinary sciences.

This review gives an elaborate overview of CDs, mainly their synthesis methods, structural and optical properties, and recent developments in various areas of application. By leveraging their versatile properties, CDs are poised to play a transformative role in the development of sustainable technologies and innovative solutions across multiple scientific domains.

Numerous publications detailing the synthesis of a broad range of CDs using both traditional chemical processes and environmentally acceptable green technologies have surfaced in recent years. All of these approaches aim to enhance the synthesis of CDs by employing methods like streamlining the entire process and using biological reagents or products, which reduce the number of steps involved and make the synthesis more economical and environmentally friendly [17,18]. The several precursors and synthesis techniques that give CDs their varied shapes are closely related. The two primary categories of CD synthesis strategies are "top-down" and "bottom-up" strategies. Top-down techniques like laser ablation (LA), arc discharge (AcD), electrochemical reactions, and plasma treatment all make use of carbon nanomaterials. Examples of bottom-up approaches include chemical oxidation, reverse micelle processes, microwave-based techniques, supported synthetic procedures, pyrolytic processes, and template methods [19–22]. Fig. 1 below illustrates both top-down and bottom-up methods for synthesizing CDs.

Thermal and combustion tactics, as well as template strategies, are typically used in the bottom-up strategies of CD preparation. When a precursor is heated outside, its carbon atoms reorganize and become carbonized, a process known as combustion or thermal strategies. All carbon-containing precursors, such as citric acid, phenylenediamine, acetaldehyde, phloroglucinol, melamine, and natural biomass, can theoretically be utilized to produce CDs through a combustion or thermal process. Depending on where the heat is coming from, the synthesis procedures can be divided into numerous groups, including plasma jet, hydrothermal/solvothermal treatment, direct pyrolysis, microwave, ultrasonic therapy, and magnetic hyperthermia [23–26]. Many byproducts are formed during the process of direct pyrolysis, which produces CDs in a solvent-free solution with incomplete carbonization. On the other hand, direct pyrolysis is a more complicated and involved process than microwave manufacturing, which can reach temperatures beyond 1000 °C in less than a minute. The ultrafast heating speeds do not depend on the thermal conductivity of the carbon materials because they are produced by matter interacting with electromagnetic radiation [27,28].

CDs are generated via the hydrothermal/solvothermal process, which combines elevated vapor pressures and temperatures in the reaction solution. As a result, solvent characteristics, including boiling point and polarity, are crucial in the production of CDs. The near ultraviolet fluorescence exhibited by the majority of CDs prepared in aqueous solutions was a consequence of the strong polarity of water, which posed a difficulty in dissolving them in conjugated organic solvents. As a consequence, low-polarity reaction solvents are likely to produce CDs exhibiting long-wavelength emission. Additionally, the reaction time and catalyst type have a significant bearing on the properties of CDs [29–31]. Moreover, there have been reports of hydrothermal reactions generated by vacuum, which emphasize the uniform dimensions of CDs. Using a plasma jet to create a chemically active environment filled with charged particles and excited neutral species is an alternative method for quickly producing CDs. Although still in its early stages, this approach is highly fascinating for its potential in large-scale implementation because it allows for fast and precise processing of materials [32,33]. Self-assembly, alternatively referred to as bottom-up synthesis, entails the integration of minute constituents, including atoms, molecules, and smaller particles, to generate nanoparticles. However, these methods are undermined by the utilization of potentially dangerous substances, elevated expenses, ecological toxicity, heightened levels of consumption, and protracted by-products during response times. Several chemical processes involve the use of hazardous reducing agents, including hydrazine, sodium borohydride (NaBH4), ethylene glycol, and sodium citrate. Nevertheless, the aforementioned approaches frequently result in substandard particle sizes and poor control over size distribution [34–37]. To mitigate this, supplementary capping agents are required, including dendrimers, surfactants (e.g., sodium dodecyl sulfate, cetyltrimethylammonium bromide [CTAB], phospholipids, and gemini surfactants), as well as capping agents such as polyethylene glycol, polyvinylalcohol, ascorbic acid, polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP), ascorbic acid, and gemini surfactants. These agents serve the purpose of steric stabilization and prevent the aggregation of nanoparticles. Biosynthesis is a sustainable process in which metal atoms aggregate and transform into nanoparticles and clusters via a bottom-up mechanism [38]. Chemical reduction is associated with the biosynthetic concept, as it produces nanoparticles from environmentally friendly substances as opposed to expensive and hazardous chemicals. To distinguish between the reduction of nanoparticles utilizing green synthesis and a conventional wet-chemistry technique, researchers implemented adverse reaction monitoring. The reduced cytotoxicity and phytotoxicity of green nanoparticles in comparison to moist chemical nanoparticles suggest that they are safe for extensive use in biomedical applications [39,40]. Bacteria, yeast, plants, algae, and fungi are examples of biological agents that occur in the environment. Constantly altering the structure and appearance of synthesized nanoparticles are the properties that define living organisms. The diversity of biological entities has resulted in an assortment of sizes and shapes of nanoparticles, which function as a blueprint for the fabrication of nanoparticles. The production of nanoparticles can be achieved through the manipulation of various parameters, including synthesis temperature, acidity, aging effects of plant extracts, and simmering times. Biogenic synthesis offers several benefits, including a profusion of biomolecules, cost-effectiveness, stability, non-toxic composition, and uncomplicated and secure methodologies [41]. A variety of bottom-up approaches to CD synthesis are detailed in Table 1 .

To produce CDs, a bulk carbon-based target (such as oriented pyrolytic graphite and activated carbon) was ablated using arc-discharging or laser in certain initial investigations. These methods involve the reorganization of carbon atoms that have been disassembled from precursor carbon precursors in bulk using gas plasma produced within a hermetic reactor [52,53]. However, while the latter utilizes a high-energy laser discharge to produce gas plasma, the former operates on an exceptionally high applied voltage. CDs produced by these techniques generally exhibit limited fluorescence, necessitating additional passivation of the polymer. A small number of articles, however, detail the process of laser ablation of a carbon powder solution in various organic solvents, which results in the production of surface-passivated CDs in a single phase. The efficacy of CDs has been demonstrated to be conditional on the reaction conditions, including parameters such as emission time and wavelength ablation source [54,55].

Conversely, oxidative cleavage, also known as oxidation cutting, is a more prevalent strategy. By employing a potent oxidant, this technique reduces carbon targets in abundance, including carbon fiber, CX-72 carbon black, candle soot, and Super P, to the size of carbon dots. The distinct categorization of oxidative cleavage processes—chemical oxidation and electrochemical oxidation—is determined by the various oxidation conditions [56,57]. A variety of assisted techniques, including hydrothermal/solvothermal treatments and reflux, are employed during the chemical oxidation process to accelerate its progression. Edge sites may form when the oxidant—typically a mixture of HNO3/H2SO4—severs the carbon-carbon bonds in the bulk carbon target using a "scissors" mechanism. The bulk carbon target is oxidatively cleaved into CDs at high redox potentials during the electrochemical oxidation process by utilizing it as the working electrode [58]. The voltage that is supplied is strong enough to cause oxidation of water, which produces oxygen and hydroxyl radicals that function as electrochemical "scissors" to create CDs. The applied voltage and solution pH often control the type of CDs during this procedure. The size of the CDs is inversely correlated with the applied voltage, and CDs produced in an alkaline solution are likely to have benign optical characteristics and high crystallinity [59,60]. Different top-down methods for the synthesis of CDs can be seen in Table 2 .

Because of their diverse nanostructure, CDs are challenging to characterize and classify with accuracy. Early literature used the terms "carbogenic dots" and "fluorescent nanoparticles" to allude to CDs. We consider carbonized polymer dots, graphitic carbon nitride quantum dots, graphene quantum dots, carbon nanodots, and carbon quantum dots to be the five classes of CDs that can be classified broadly according to the diversity of their carbon core. (Fig. 2 ).

Quantum dots of carbon are an innovative category of carbon nanomaterials measuring less than 10 nm. The initial acquisition of these substances occurred in 2004 during the preparative electrophoresis process employed to filter single-walled carbon nanotubes, and in 2006 during the laser ablation of cement and graphite particles. Carbon-based quantum dots have emerged as a noteworthy constituent of the nanocarbon community owing to their benign, abundant, and cost-effective characteristics, which have contributed to their captivating attributes [76,77]. In general, it was believed that carbon had poor water solubility and weak fluorescence. Carbon-based quantum dots, or carbon nanolights, have garnered a lot of interest because of their strong brightness and superior solubility.

In contrast to the aforementioned CDs, "carbonized polymer dots (CPDs)" are carbonic nanoparticles formed through the aggregation or cross-linking of monomers or linear polymers. The enhanced fluorescence observed in carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) is primarily attributed to the carbonized nucleus of these dots, as opposed to conventional polymer dots. Certain carbon core subclasses of CPDs have been identified, including the paracrystalline carbon structure composed of microscopic carbon clusters interspersed with polymer frameworks, the highly dehydrated polymer framework that crosslinks and coils, and wholly carbonized cores [78]. The degree of carbonization exhibited by CPDs is highly dependent on the precursor's properties and the reaction parameters. It is also possible to create CPDs by adding polymer molecules to other CDs. Because of their crosslinked structure, CPDs often exhibit good stability in the presence of pH, ionic strength, and UV radiation. They therefore hold great promise as a material for a range of uses, such as in vivo protein, gene, and medication delivery systems [79].

Less than 10 nm in diameter single-layer graphene is known as a graphene quantum dot (GQD). However, certain GQDs may possess a restricted number of atomic layers, functional groups on the boundaries, or inner layer imperfections as a result of less-than-ideal preparation conditions. By varying their thickness, the physicochemical properties of GQDs, including absorbance, can be drastically altered [59,80]. Moreover, similar to complete graphite, graphitic fragments, including graphite quantum dots (GQDs), possess distinct diffraction planes corresponding to graphitic inter-layer spacing and graphitic in-plane lattice spacing. However, the graphite lattice spacing of GQDs can be altered to suit specific application requirements through the doping of heteroatoms. Additionally, the fundamental properties of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are influenced by the "edge effect," a significant factor in their efficacy [81,82].

Due to the graphene-like two-dimensional structure, graphitic carbon nitride quantum dots (g-CNQDs) are seldom regarded independently, since they are often considered counterparts to GQDs. However, because of their significantly varied elemental composition and nanostructure, this concept requires modification.

The more stable nanostructure of graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) at ambient settings sets it apart from other allotropes of carbon nitride. G-CNQDs are made up of tri-s-triazine units connected by amino groups, just like the 2D g-C3N4 sheet, and they display periodic vacancies in their lattice structure [83,84]. The unique "poly(tri-s-triazine)" framework of g-CNQDs, which possesses many extremely defective amine groups (-NH or -NH2) on the terminal edges, is responsible for the remarkable catalytic and other features of these compounds. Due to a reduced quantum size effect akin to that of GQDs, the large-sized g-C3N4 demonstrated little absorption of visible light to produce photoelectrons [85]. Even though g-CNQD development and applications are currently the subjects of fewer publications than GQDs, CNQDs have a promising future because of their sound nature.

The words "carbon nanodots (CNDs)" and "CQDs" have been used interchangeably in several articles. CNDs are quasi-spherical carbonic nanoparticles with an amorphous structural core, in contrast to CQDs. For instance, a prominent hump located at around ∼ 2h = 26} in the X-ray diffraction profile suggests that the crystal structure of CNDs is unclear. Although not much has been investigated thus far, CNDs can be used in the future because of their luminous features and relatively low cost [7,86]. Based on the structural structure of the carbon core, it is utterly nonsensical and unscientific to differentiate between carbon nanodots and carbon quantum dots. Either of these can be used to characterize the spherical CDs.

The most intriguing aspects of CD research are their light emission characteristics, which is why they have drawn interest. Research into CD photoluminescence (PL) and up-converted photoluminescence (UCPL), two emission properties that are accompanied by anti-Stokes shift and Stokes shift, respectively, has rapidly grown. Due to their diverse luminous properties, CDs find widespread application in the fields of photoelectricity, energy, and biology. The wavelength of PL emission is greater than that of the excitation wavelength. Luminescence and fluorescence are classified as subcategories of CD photoluminescence, [7,87,88]. As a result of the emission wave possessing a greater energy than the excitation wave, the emission wavelength in UCPL is shorter than the excitation wavelength. This distinguishes UCPL from standard PL. Early investigations on the UCPL of CDs utilized monochromatic light from a fluorescence spectrophotometer, powered by a xenon lamp, due to the absence of a filter. Due to the constant coexistence of UCPL in the λ wavelength excitation radiation, it was consistently misidentified as the second-order diffraction of λ/2 wavelength induced by conventional PL in this particular case [89,90].

When CDs are less than the Bohr radius of the exciton, they demonstrate the quantum confinement effect (QCE). The transition of the valence band and conduction band from continuous energy levels to discrete energy levels occurs due to the quantum confinement effect (QCE) when the three-dimensional dimensions of the material approach the nanometer scale. As a consequence, the band gap expands, sending the substance into the ultraviolet–visible region and yielding a substantial increase in the fluorescent quantum yield (QY) [31]. The predominant source of fluorescence observed in certain CDs is the Quantum Confinement Effect (QCE) induced by conjugated π electrons within sizable π-domains that contain few surface functional groups. The fluorescence center for carbon-core states is considered to be the band gap of conjugated π-domains. A greater redshift is observed in the emission peak as the extent of the conjugated π-domain of CDs increases, accompanied by a decrease in the band gap [91,92]. The valence band of the CDs becomes distinct from the conduction band due to the presence of constrained-size conjugated π-domains. By facilitating the "direct recombination" of electrons and holes, this separation enables conduction band electrons to transition directly to an unoccupied state in the valence band, thereby inducing band gap fluorescence. The π-electron state of the sp2 carbon clusters, which are dispersed throughout the sp3 carbon backbone, exerts an influence on the optical properties of carbon dots (CDs) [93,94]. The fluorescence observed during electron-hole pair transitions is a consequence of the influence of the σ and σ∗ electron energy levels in the sp2 carbon cluster on the σ and σ∗ states in the sp3 carbon skeleton. Potentially manipulating the color emitted by CDs is possible through the adjustment of the band gap via modifications in the dimensions of conjugated π-domains [95].

Since CDs' electrochemical characteristics are closely linked to both their surface state and carbon core, they are important in a variety of applications. In particular, when compared to a 2D graphene sheet, the surface energy trap and quantum confinement effect have a greater impact on CD electrochemical characteristics. CDs can serve as both electron donors and acceptors in a range of applications where electrons and holes are energized by photons, originate, and then are transferred to other species. To date, a great deal of work has been done to investigate the connection between their electrical characteristics and carbon core [8,96]. It has been demonstrated that the size of GQDs influences their ability to trap individual electrons between them and their surface. It's possible that surface functionalization and heteroatom doping will improve CD electrochemical performance more than carbon core design. The introduction of electron-donating heteroatoms, such as nitrogen, can theoretically enhance heterogeneous electron transfer (HET), whereas doping with electron-removing heteroatoms, such as halogens, leads in a decrease in HET rates. Because the conductive sp2 carbon network is disrupted when there are more oxygen functional groups on CDs, electron transmission may also be slowed down. Due to their superior cooperative effect, dual- and multi-element co-doping has become the subject of an increasing number of research investigating its impact on CD electron transport. Additionally, metal dopants can greatly improve CD charge transfer [59,97].

As demonstrated by the economical synthesis techniques, CDs are feasible options for an extensive range of practical applications. In the realm of electrochemical applications, CDs possess remarkable attributes such as exceptional electrical conductivity, an extensive surface area, a wide variety of electrochemical active sites, compatibility with various materials, robust flexibility, and resistance to environmental stress. By combining CDs with conductive polymers and metal oxides, electrode materials in supercapacitors and lithium/sodium/potassium-ion batteries can have their specific capacity, cycle stability, and rate performance enhanced [98,99]. Conductivities and electrocatalytic activity for oxygen reduction reaction (ORR), oxygen evolution reaction (OER), and hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) in metal-air batteries, fuel cells, and water/carbon dioxide electrolysis can be enhanced by incorporating heteroatom dopants into carbon dots. In order to optimize electrocatalytic performance, it is necessary to modify reaction conditions to influence the dimensions and integration of functional groups onto the surface of carbon spots [100,101].

CDs present three fundamental advantages in the domains of electrochemical energy storage and catalysis: CDs exhibit desirable properties that support the creation of electron-hole pairs to improve electrical conductivity, dope heteroatoms, and modifying surface functional groups to enhance electrochemical performance and stability. Additionally, they provide a significant surface area and exhibit exceptional wettability. CDs may be utilized as precursors, inducers, or templates to generate substances with unique structures [31,102]. CDs that are inexpensive have the potential to replace graphene and other expensive carbon nanostructures. Sintering can be employed to generate a conductive network in graphite microregions by subjecting it to elevated temperatures and thereby expanding the layer spacing. CDs are compatible with a variety of systems. CDs can be generated via hydro/solvo-thermal, microwave-assisted synthesis, high-temperature carbonization, electrophoretic deposition (EPD), electrochemical deposition, or direct incorporation into synthesis processes [103,104].

The primary reason dispersibility is a critical characteristic of CDs utilized in industrial manufacturing and application is their functional groups and surface structure. Numerous studies have provided evidence that the precursors and preparation methods employed in CDs dictate their hydrophilic and hydrophobic characteristics. The favorable hydrophilicity and dispersibility of the majority of CDs can be attributed to the formation of oxygen-containing functional groups in the precursor during synthesis [105,106]. Furthermore, a reciprocal conversion between hydrophilicity and hydrophobicity in CDs can be achieved through modifications to the surface functional groups. By synthesizing hydrophilic CDs in a single-pot hydrothermal process, the significance of functional groups in determining the hydrophilicity of the final product was illustrated [107].

Fluorescence must be generated via surface oxidation or additional passivation for CDs to be produced using any preparation method; thus, the oxygenated groups on their surface render them predominately hydrophilic. CDs were manufactured by the Ding group through an electrochemical process in 2007. By employing chemical vapor deposition on a carbon paper substrate, multiwalled carbon nanotubes were produced [108]. Having been appropriately trimmed, the paper was inserted as the working electrode into a Teflon casing. Prior to dissolving the residual solid in water, CDs were initially produced in acetonitrile solutions that were purified by evaporation. Following this, additional synthesis techniques utilizing bulk materials or organic precursors have been developed via bottom-up or top-down initiatives [109]. In contrast to ligand exchange, the encapsulation process generally spared the quantum yield. CDs are dispersible in various polar solvents and exhibit solution-dependent fluorescence due to the abundance of hydrophilic groups present on their surfaces. Cyclodextrins are amenable to innumerable applications, including drug delivery, biochemistry, fluorescence probes, and diagnostics, due to the hydrophilic and stable nature of functional groups when exposed to aqueous environments [110].

While most CDs discovered are hydrophilic, researchers are exploring ways to create hydrophobic CDs with unique properties such as self-cleaning, chemical reaction suppression, and water-resistance for industrial use. In 2008, the Giannelis group created carbogenic quantum dots using pyrolysis techniques [111]. Depending on the surface modifiers, nanomaterials that are dispersible in various solvents can be produced using citric acid or 4-aminoantipyrine as precursors. In the same year, a novel collection of disseminated or supported carbogenic nanoparticles was acquired through the thermal oxidation of pre-exchanged zeolite hosts that were utilized to embellish the outer surfaces of zeolite crystals. Each particular derivative can be readily dispersed in organic solvents at high concentrations (50 mg mL−1) due to its organophilic corona [112,113]. A variety of organic solvents, including acetone, ethanol, chloroform, toluene, THF, NMP, hexane, cyclohexane, DMF, and acetonitrile, were found to be effective in dispersing hydrophobic CDs. The hydrophobic characteristics exhibited by both fragmented and unfragmented PF-68 are due to the presence of hydrophobic long chains and reduced surface hydroxyl groups. The process being proposed bore striking resemblance to other organophilic carbon dots, where passivation was facilitated by long-chain amines subsequent to the formation of amide bonds on the carbon dot. Hydrophobic cyclodextrins are increasingly being investigated via direct synthesis and phase transfer from polar to non-polar solvents [114].

One of CDs' most important advantages over semiconductor quantum dots has been recognized as being "low toxicity," which contributes to their potential for application in nanoscale dimensions and bio-imaging. Several studies indicate that at moderate concentrations, CDs cause non-negligible cytotoxicity to a variety of cell types. It is important to remember that the term "low" is used extremely loosely. In fact, when concentrations of CDs exceed an optimal level, they cause holes and shrinkage to appear on the surface of cells, which inhibits in vivo and in vitro cell growth. Furthermore, outside solutions also have the ability to change the toxicity of CDs [115,116]. Studies on cytotoxicity are typically conducted in the dark, which is inconsistent with their real-world use. There has been enough research on the toxicity of light-irradiated CDs, but there are growing signs that the reactive oxygen species they produce could be detrimental to bacteria or cells. The widespread photodegradation phenomenon for CDs will exacerbate photo-induced CD radical generation (such as hydroxyl and alkyl radicals). According to analysis, CDs will photodegrade more quickly at higher temperatures, pH values, light intensities, and shorter wavelengths [117]. Properties of CDs can be seen in Fig. 3 .

CDs exhibit emission-color tuning, high quantum yields, non-blinking behavior, and great photo and chemical stability, defining their luminescence features. These characteristics facilitate the creation of extremely sensitive nanosensors that can be used in solution or immobilized. CDs exert an increasing influence on analytical research in the near future, given the aforementioned characteristics they possess. For bioimaging applications that target specific cell types and compartments, further research is required to develop methods for CD production and functionalization [89,118]. The enhancement of nanosensor sensitivity and selectivity, alongside the development of lifetime-based detection methods, should be the primary objectives of research pertaining to analytical and bioanalytical applications. It is pivotal that fluorescence-based techniques employ low-energy excitation sources, given that CDs are highly suitable for the analysis of biological samples. In addition to the development of multiphoton-based analytical techniques, research on CDs should prioritize the production of nanoparticles that are stimulated by red or near-infrared light [118,119]. The findings suggest that CDs are a highly adaptable material that can be easily modified and affixed to solid supports in order to attain and maintain the desired reactivity required for chemical or biochemical analysis, despite the limited instances in which they have been utilized as nanosensors for chemical analysis.

Bioimaging is an imaging technique that enables direct observation of biological processes in action. It is frequently employed to gather data regarding the three-dimensional morphology of an object as perceived from the exterior, devoid of any physical intervention. CDs are utilized for bioimaging due to their biocompatibility and fluorescence emissions. For identification or therapeutic purposes, solvents containing CDs can be injected into living things to create in vivo images [120]. CDs coupled with organic dyes are an effective fluorescent H2S probe. When H2S is present, organic dye-conjugated CDs' blue emission may turn green. Therefore, using a fluorescence microscope, organic-dye-conjugated CDs can reveal variations in the physiologically significant rates of H2S. A few of the CDs have been utilized for phototherapies and cancer diagnosis [118].

Biocompatibility, low cost, fluorescence with minimal photobleaching and photoblinking, and simplicity of production have all contributed to the increased use of CDs in sensing and imaging. A considerable number of C-dot sensing systems employ analyte-induced fluorescence quenching. While some CDs demonstrate a specific affinity for particular analytes (e.g., quercetin, Cu2+, Fe3+, ascorbic acid, glucose, hydroxyl radicals, tartrazine, iodide), others necessitate augmentation with cognate recognition elements (e.g., antibodies and aptamers) to target proteins with precision [96,121]. The optimization of carbon dot (CD) functionalization is of utmost importance to improve their selectivity, sensitivity, and biocompatibility, as it significantly influences their fluorescence and hydrophilic characteristics. Like numerous other nanoparticles, CDs are capable of penetrating cells via a variety of mechanisms, including endocytosis. CDs are localized within cells according to the properties of their surfaces, which include charge, hydrophilicity, and surface ligand [8,96]. Moreover, their dimensions play a pivotal role. To attain optimal sensitivity, it is imperative to impede autofluorescence that emanates from the cell matrix. The minimal toxicity of CDs has been confirmed through the utilization of an MTT experiment. CDs are commonly eliminated from the body through the kidneys, liver, and bladder following ingestion or injection. The impact on blood cell count and organ functions is negligible. On account of their biocompatibility, photostability, and luminosity, CDs have gained popularity for imaging applications [122].

Because of its great biocompatibility, high water solubility, nontoxicity, good photostability, and adaptability in alteration, CDs have also been employed as biosensors. Visual monitoring of cellular copper, glucose, pH, H2O2 trace levels, and nucleic acid can be achieved with biosensors based on CD-based materials. Nucleic acid lateral flow tests are a helpful application. The amplicons' detecting tags are recognized by the respective CDs' fluorescence patterns and antibodies that correspond with them. The degree to which CDs' fluorescence can be effectively influenced by factors like pH, local polarity, and the existence of metal ions in a solution raises the possibility of using them in nanosensing applications, such as pollution analysis. applications of carbon dots in sensing technology, specifically graphene and quantum dots [8,96,118]. CDs are currently being studied in an increasing number of applications due to the growing interest in carbon dot-based materials chemistry among scientists. Given their distinctive solid-state fluorescence and phosphorescence properties, CDs are anticipated to serve as a medium in the domain of security, encompassing anticounterfeiting signatures and biometric identification.

Rapid worldwide industrialization and ongoing population increase have led to air and water pollution, resulting in the spread of pathogens and infectious diseases. All of these substances have several detrimental impacts on human health, even at extremely low levels. CDs, as innovative fluorescent nanomaterials and nanoenzymes, have significant promise for detecting environmental contaminants by fluorescence and colorimetric methods. Although the CDs-based colorimetric method offers advantages over the fluorescence method in pollution detection, it is seldom utilized since it is easily influenced by external factors. Like other nanozymes, CDs-based nanozymes colorimetry is often effective for simple samples but may be hindered by a decrease in activity if interfering chemicals are absorbed onto its surfaces [123,124]. A polymer can be utilized to modify CDs to decrease non-specific adsorption and limit the loss of catalytic activity simultaneously. The colorimetric and fluorescent dual-mode assay is a suitable option for sensitive and convenient environmental contaminant detection. It offers a very sensitive fluorescence assay and enables easy observation of targets without the need for additional aids. These sensors can detect target contaminants conveniently, sensitively, and selectively using both colorimetric and fluorescence methods. Yet, the intricate fabrication process and expensive nature continue to limit their extensive use [96,118,125].

Membrane water treatment technologies are widely used worldwide. CDs can be used in membrane-based separation processes. CDs may be effortlessly incorporated into membrane substrates because of their numerous surface functional groups, extremely small size, and outstanding water dispersibility, unlike graphite oxide sheets. Recent research has shown that modifying the substrate layer or entire membranes by adding CDs can improve membrane stability and function. The adsorption-based methodology is considered a versatile water treatment method that is widely used for removing environmental pollutants due to its quickness, affordability, and effectiveness [126,127]. Traditionally, activated carbon is commonly utilized as an adsorbent due to its high adsorption capability for a variety of contaminants. However, the cost and complexity of producing and regenerating activated carbon limits its widespread use. Graphene and carbon-based nanomaterials such as CDs have been developed as alternatives to traditional adsorbent materials. CDs possess a multitude of advantages as adsorbents in contrast to graphene substances. CDs provide a multitude of sites for surface adsorption due to their minuscule dimensions and vast specific surface area [128]. Additionally, CDs can be easily manufactured through a simple process that requires scarce and inexpensive raw materials, as well as no specialized apparatus. In addition, the material composed of CDs incorporates several hydrophilic functional groups, which obviate the necessity for additional alterations that would augment its hydrophilic properties and reactivity [129]. This advantage is critical given that the adsorption of contaminants by CDs may be attributed to these functional groups.

In recent years, there has been increased scholarly attention directed towards supercapacitors (SCs) due to their superior power density, prolonged cycle life, and rapid charge and discharge capabilities. While submerged in the electrolyte, the electrodes of a supercapacitor are separated electrically by a diaphragm. Based on their energy storage mechanisms, two varieties of SCs can be distinguished: double-layer capacitors (DLCs), which store energy via adsorption-desorption, and pseudocapacitors, which are substances that store hydrogen via a redox process. The advancement of practical applications for SCs is impeded by their comparatively lower energy density when compared to conventional batteries. To increase the energy density of SCs and develop novel electrode materials with superior cycle stability and high capacity, a substantial amount of effort has been devoted to this end [130,131]. Extremely high capacity and maximal efficiency are characteristics of CDs-based electrodes, which are further enhanced by their large number of active sites, high surface area, significant wettability in a variety of solvents, and tunable bandgap. Carbon/graphene quantum dots, alternatively referred to as GQDs or CQDs, exhibit quantum tunneling, surface, and dimension characteristics, all while preserving the chemical stability characteristic of carbon materials. Its robust ability to adsorb electrolyte ions and its numerous development opportunities in SCs are both noteworthy attributes [8,59].

Using applied electric fields or light sources, respectively, as catalysts, photocatalysis, and electrocatalysis are the two main catalytic uses of CDs. Catalysis has been shown to have a major impact on several environmental protection and energy conversion challenges, such as the degradation of organic pollutants, the conversion of CO2 into water, and so forth. Because they are less expensive and hazardous than traditional catalysts, CDs-based catalysts have sparked greater research attention. On the one hand, because of their quantum confinement effects, pure CDs can act as catalysts directly. Colloidal CDs show promise as an oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) catalyst, however their electrocatalytic activity was marginally less than that of a Pt/C electrode [132,133].

There are two fundamental categories of LEDs derived from CDs: down-conversion LEDs and electroluminescent (charge-injection) LEDs. By depositing CDs onto a commercial UV-emitting semiconductor that functions as an excitation source, the initial type can be produced. In the literature, they are frequently described as a demonstration apparatus that can produce light across a wide spectrum of wavelengths, including WLEDs with different color temperatures. A further constraint for down-conversion CD-based WLEDs is the scarcity of red-emitting CDs with a high PL QY. To mitigate this concern, it is advantageous to integrate blue or green-emitting CDs with additional red luminophores such as rare-earth phosphors (RFPs) or quantum dots (QDs) [134,135].

CDs function as the active layer in the second category of LEDs, enabling the generation of electroluminescence via charge injection that is stimulated by an externally applied electric field. Materials utilized in these light-emitting diodes (LEDs) are subject to several restrictions, including the selection of electron-/hole-transporting layers by their energy structure, the requirement for particular HOMO-LUMO level positions, and the need to achieve a high rate of radiative recombination while minimizing nonradiative transitions. It is more difficult to manufacture electroluminescent LEDs as opposed to down-conversion LEDs. CDs-based LEDs continue to have lower efficacy than those composed of semiconductor QDs, perovskites, or organic molecules [136].

Xie et al. used pretreated cellulose to synthesize CDs in the supernatant with carbonized deposits at the bottom through a high‐temperature carbonization process. The material was used to construct for anode for sodium‐ion batteries, and it achieved a high specific capacity of 300 mAh·g−1 as well as an excellent initial coulombic efficiency (ICE) of 91 % [137]. Liu et al. developed a new strategy for synthesizing edge‐nitrogen‐rich CD pillared graphene blocks (N‐CDGB) through self‐polymerization of aniline into the graphene oxide blocks (GOBs), followed by subsequent carbonization. The unique columnar structure developed was used as the anode material for sodium‐ion batteries [138]. Zhao et al. synthesized CQDs/MoS2 composites using a facile hydrothermal method. During the synthesis, 30 min of visible light irradiation could induce an enhanced electrocatalytic HER activity with an overpotential of ∼0.125 V at 10 mA cm−2 and the Tafel slope of 45 mV·dec−1. The promoted activity of this catalyst was attributed to the fast charge‐transfer efficiency as well as the decrease of S4+ and the increase of S2− after light treatment [139]. Yang et al. synthesized nickel nanoparticle/CQD complexes (Ni/CQDs) and evaluated their electrocatalytic activity in a highly concentrated alkaline medium. The obtained Ni/CQD complexes had a good catalytic activity for HER, and their overpotential was comparable to that of Pt, with a lower Tafel slope of 98 mV·dec−1, which was attributed to the Ni–O–C interface between Ni nanoparticles and CQDs. After 1000 CV cycling, these Ni/CQDs exhibited a durable stability with a negligible current loss of 1 mA cm−2 [140].

Given their compact size and reactive nature, CDs are well suited for the visualization of latent fingerprints since they bind with the fingerprint ridge easily. One major benefit of using CDs for latent fingerprinting is that the organic and inorganic components of the fingerprint samples do not age. Fingerprints lose some of their organic and inorganic composition as people age, making them more difficult for detectives to locate. Furthermore, they found that the long-term stability of the films allowed for the use of CDs embedded in polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) to produce an extremely detailed image of the fingerprint. Citric acid has been observed to produce positive effects when used with diethylenetriamine as a carbon source [6].

Photodynamic therapy (PDT) and photothermal therapy (PTT) are novel therapeutic methodologies that employ laser light to address ailments including cancer. PTT typically employs NIR photo absorbers to generate localized heat and eradicate target cells, such as cancer cells. In contrast to traditional cancer treatments like invasive surgery, chemotherapy, or radiotherapy, this method offers distinct advantages including minimal invasiveness, exceptional specificity, and precise temporal selectivity. To combat the earliest stage of cancer metastasis, PTT is capable of directly destroying cancer cells at the primary tumor site or in adjacent lymph nodes that have undergone localized metastasis [136,141]. Additionally, it can be combined with other established therapeutic approaches to target cancer cells in metastatic sites. Numerous research organizations have examined the therapeutic efficacy of photothermal therapy as a potential treatment for cancer in recent years. The efficacy of photothermal therapy is notably predicated on the transformation of light into heat when photothermal agents, particularly nanodimensional agents, are present. As photothermal agents, numerous nanoscale materials, including graphene, gold nanostructures, and graphene oxide, have been implemented. Scholars are currently engaged in the development of novel photothermal agents that are affordable, as these materials are difficult to synthesize and require expensive production methods [142,143].

CDs have characteristics that make them ideal for extended cell marking and monitoring. They possess a wide excitation band and good photostability, offering benefits compared to fluorescent dyes. In numerous experiments, CDs have demonstrated negligible cytotoxicity, suggesting that they remain biocompatible even when exposed to high concentrations. Their low cytotoxicity allows for their efficient and secure application as agents for tracing cells [144]. Cellular assimilation of CDs improves their efficacy in optical cell tracking, which is not a detrimental process. The multicolor emission of CDs is an advantageous property in the field of cell labeling, as it enables the flexible differentiation and tracking of different categories of cells. Protective or functional coatings are of the utmost importance on CDs to enable precise tracing of specific cell types via selective cell labeling [145].

CDs can effectively transport genetic material into cells. Targeting ligands, including peptides or antibodies, can be added to boost gene transport efficiency by enhancing specific cellular uptake. Beneficially charged compounds, such as chitosan or polyethyleneimine (PEI), can be used to modify CD surfaces to facilitate the condensation and binding of negatively charged DNA or RNA molecules. This procedure serves to protect the molecules from annihilation and facilitates their transportation into cells. The intense fluorescence emission exhibited by CDs, among other notable optical properties, can be harnessed to visually monitor the progression of gene delivery. By conjugating fluorescent pigments or quantum dots to CDs, it is possible to evaluate the transfection efficiency of the transmitted genes and monitor their intracellular pathway [146,147].

CDs are widely used in biomedicine for purposes like drug delivery, fluorescent tracers, and monitoring drug release due to their lack of toxicity and compatibility with living organisms. The application of CDs as photosensitizers in photodynamic treatment for the killing of cancer cells serves as proof of this. The literature is replete with instances of CDs being used to provide a wide range of medications to both humans and animals. The primary advantages of this kind of CD-based drug administration are the carriers' inherent nontoxicity, PL, and biocompatibility [148]. Compact size and increased surface area allow CDs to be taken up by cells as carriers more quickly. As a result, the carrier molecule is least negatively impacted. The medicine doxorubicin has been released with success using it [149]. The application of CDs can be seen in Fig. 4 . Table 3 summarizes the diverse applications of carbon dots (C-Dots) across multiple domains, highlighting their potential in biomedical, sensing, energy, food technology, optical technologies, and environmental applications.

Because CDs are customizable, adaptable, and multipurpose, a wide range of vibrant uses are possible. Applications appear to be lagging behind research on the structures and characteristics of carbon dots thus far. Even after much research, there are still important issues with CDs that need to be resolved. Producing CDs with clearly defined morphologies and structures, as well as having a thorough comprehension of the intricate reaction mechanism of preparations, continue to be formidable challenges. It is still necessary to quantitatively clarify the structure-property relationship of CDs, particularly in connection to photogenerated charge separations, toxicity, and biocompatibility. By utilizing the exceptional design potential of CDs and the synergism between carbon and organics, one might expect enhanced performance and novel applications. Crystalline CDs seem to have advantages over amorphous ones in terms of both structure and properties. CDs may not truly exhibit a quantum confinement effect, hence it is important to exercise caution when interpreting their "quantum effect" despite their nanoscale size. Despite being known for their low toxicity, CDs have the potential to be released into the environment and to disperse and accumulate in aquatic ecosystems. Thus, it is desirable to conduct extensive research on the harmful consequences of CDs. Due in large part to their diverse photoelectrochemical qualities, plentiful raw material sources, tunable architectures, and strong biocompatibility, CDs exhibit a wealth of application potential. However, the basis for research on sustainable applications should be the unique characteristics and nature of CDs, which make them irreplaceable in certain applications. Broad and universal CDs don't have any practical use, in some ways.

Over the past decade, there has been a significant increase in interest in material science, environmental remediation, and medical applications for CDs [152], a distinctive carbon-based material known for its outstanding physicochemical characteristics. This overview focuses on the most recent advancements in carbon-based materials, highlighting various synthesis methods and the uses of CDs in various fields. CDs will be both affordable and environmentally beneficial if they are made from waste carbon materials. Among the different approaches, the green synthesis strategy for CDs is the most recommended one since it will reduce pollution from solid waste entering the environment. Although many CD synthesis techniques have been described to date, their large-scale manufacture still faces difficulties due to their simplicity and inability to produce high yields. Because of their biocompatibility and nontoxic properties, CDs may find use as drug carriers in targeted drug delivery applications in the future. They may also find use in the agricultural sector, where surface functionalization may increase their effectiveness and specificity. In summary, CD development is still in its early stages, and more study is required in the area before practical applications can be made.

Carbon Dots (CDs) have emerged as one of the most promising classes of nanomaterials due to their unmatched imaging sensitivity, deep penetration in the long-wavelength region, multifunctional surfaces, and environmentally friendly, cost-effective synthetic methods. Over the past decade, significant progress has been made in understanding and harnessing their properties for diverse applications. However, critical challenges remain that hinder their full potential. The mechanisms underlining the fluorescence behavior of CDs, and their interaction with various micro-structures are yet to be identified. The primary reason is that currently, there are no strong experimental methods designed to correlate structural features and photoluminescence properties. Production of CDs so far remains a laboratory-based synthesis and limited progress made towards industrial scale-up at high yield. Some practical challenges that need to be addressed include low sensitivity, poor target specificity, and lack of multi-component simultaneous detection research. Promising the solution to such challenges are innovations in the design and development of biomass-derived CDs. With the focus on developing more sustainable synthesis methods, and by going deeper in mechanistic studies, this would open up full-potential exploitation of the transformative innovation across various domains of science and industry.
